input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical twin is a lab‑scale gantry crane used as a research and education exemplar. It consists of the crane mechanism itself together with an electronic controller that drives the crane. The system operates in a laboratory environment for material‑handling experiments and provides a simple, well‑instrumented hardware platform whose geometry and kinematics are easy to model, making it suitable for demonstrating digital‑twin concepts.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The digital twin provides (1) optimal control services that compute control commands for the crane, (2) historical data logging that records sensor and actuator data over time, (3) data visualization services that render the crane state and performance metrics, and (4) continuous validation services that compare physical measurements with the kinematic model to detect deviations. These services target researchers and educators using the exemplar.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The implementation uses only freely available and commonly used software. The digital side includes CAD models for geometric representation and a kinematic model for motion prediction, supported by open‑source tools for modelling, data storage, and service deployment; no proprietary frameworks are cited.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The gantry crane Digital Twin follows an ad‑hoc architecture modelled with the C4 approach and uses containerization to package its software components, deploying them on virtual machines without a commercial DT framework; component coordination is handled within this container‑based deployment.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin constellation comprises a geometric CAD model of the gantry crane structure, a kinematic model that describes the Cartesian motion of the cart and the hoisting mechanism, and a behavioral model that captures the dynamics of the swing and load handling. Sensor data (e.g., swing angle, wind speed) are ingested from the physical system and stored in a time‑series database (InfluxDB/TimescaleDB) accessed via a Data Service. Recorded datasets are packaged in virtual‑machine images for reproducibility. The Simulation Service consumes the CAD and kinematic models to generate predicted trajectories, which are then compared with live measurements. Model components are linked through the twin’s orchestration layer, which maps physical entities to their corresponding digital representations for synchronized operation.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The physical actuator suite consists of two electric motors each driven by a dedicated motor controller: one motor controls the horizontal movement of the cart along the gantry, and the second motor drives the vertical hoist. At the end of the hoist an electromagnet is mounted to pick up and release the small container pucks. Actuation commands are dispatched over the ActiveMQ message broker, enabling remote control from the twin’s control layer. Safety constraints include limits on cart speed, hoist height, and electromagnet activation to prevent overload, all enforced by the controller firmware.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing hardware includes a high‑resolution swing‑angle encoder mounted on the hoist to measure pendulum motion, and an anemometer placed near the crane to record ambient wind speed. Both sensors sample at rates sufficient for real‑time control (typically tens of hertz) and transmit timestamped readings via MQTT/ActiveMQ to the InfluxDB time‑series store. The sensors provide accuracy levels appropriate for trajectory validation (angle resolution in sub‑degree range, wind speed accuracy ±0.1 m/s).
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: Model fidelity is maintained by a Continuous Validation Service that continuously compares live sensor measurements of executed trajectories against simulated trajectories generated by the kinematic model. The service computes distance metrics (e.g., RMS error) and flags deviations exceeding a predefined threshold, triggering alerts or model re‑calibration. Validation incorporates uncertainty quantification from sensor noise and actuator tolerances, and employs automated error‑correction loops to update model parameters, ensuring the digital twin remains a trustworthy representation of the physical gantry crane.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The crane’s onboard controllers expose state information that is accessed by the Physical Crane Interface – a Python program that connects directly to the controllers. The Interface streams the measured crane state (e.g., position, velocity, load) continuously to the Logging Application, which writes the data into a TimescaleDB database. The flow is triggered by each state update (described as “continuously”). Communication uses the Python‑level API of the interface (specific protocol not named in the documents). No explicit preprocessing, data‑quality checks, or integration mechanisms beyond the direct logging are described.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: The Control Application, a Python program, uses the Physical Crane Interface to issue control commands to the crane hardware. The Trajectory Generator Service can produce optimal movement trajectories that are consumed by the Control Application for execution. The documents do not detail command formats, validation, authentication, or acknowledgement mechanisms.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: Inter‑service communication within the digital twin relies on an MQTT broker for signaling between services (Trajectory Generator, Simulation, Continuous Validation, etc.). The broker operates over standard MQTT/TCP. All components (Python applications, TimescaleDB, Grafana) are modeled as logical containers in the C4 diagram and are assumed to run on the same host network; the documents do not specify network topology, bandwidth, latency, redundancy, or additional security protocols.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The system consists of several logical containers: Physical Crane, Physical Crane Interface, Control Application, Logging Application, Human‑Machine Interface, plus twin containers (TimescaleDB database, Grafana dashboard, Trajectory Generator Service, Simulation Service, Continuous Validation Service, MQTT Broker). Deployment is on‑premise (no cloud or edge platform is mentioned) using container‑style abstraction rather than Docker containers. Computational and storage requirements are not quantified; scalability is limited to the described set of services, and no explicit availability or reliability provisions are detailed.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The development of the gantry crane digital twin is documented using the C4 Model methodology. A context diagram describes the system boundaries, a container diagram identifies the major software containers, and further diagrams (component, code) capture internal structure. This modeling approach is employed to visualize and describe the digital twin architecture, but the documents do not provide information on testing, quality‑assurance practices, version‑control mechanisms, or explicit evolution management strategies.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The twin provides several decision‑support services: an optimal‑control service that computes control actions for the physical system; a historical‑data logging service that stores measurements in InfluxDB; a data‑visualisation service that renders runtime visualisations for operators; and a continuous‑validation service that compares predicted and measured values and raises an alert when a configured threshold is breached, prompting operators to take corrective action. These services enable operators to monitor, analyze and react to the greenhouse or indoor‑air‑quality environments.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic correctly identifies the system as a lab-scale gantry crane used for research and education, and mentions the physical crane and controller. This is supported by the abstract which states: 'we present a lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin... The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller.' The extraction also correctly notes the operational domain (laboratory) and suitability for digital twin concepts. However, the extraction lacks specific details about the components found in the text, such as the motors, electromagnet, and sensors, or the specific rationale about geometry and kinematics being easy to model, which would make it a '5'.

The extraction accurately lists the four specific services mentioned in the abstract and detailed in Section III.A: optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. The descriptions provided for each service, such as 'compute control commands' and 'compare physical measurements,' are well-supported by the source text describing the Trajectory Generator and Continuous Validation Service.

The extraction accurately reflects the paper's emphasis on using 'freely available and commonly used software' and correctly identifies CAD and kinematic models as part of the digital side. It correctly summarizes the implementation details, noting the use of open-source tools for data storage and deployment, and the avoidance of specific proprietary frameworks, which is supported by the discussion in Sections III.B and V.E.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_2_judge.reasoning: The description states 'Not in Document'. The source text focuses on the architecture, components, and general functionality of the gantry crane digital twin but does not provide details on synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity.

The description states 'Not in Document'. The source text describes a single physical gantry crane and its digital twin instance. It mentions potential future extensions involving a harbor system and a ship twin, but does not define a current structure of multiple instances or decentralized architectures.

The description correctly identifies the ad-hoc architecture and the use of the C4 model ('we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and describe the architecture'). It also correctly identifies the lack of a commercial DT framework ('we utilize a more or less ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework'). The mention of containerization is supported by the text ('For ease of deployment, the Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker'). However, the extraction incorrectly states that components are deployed on 'virtual machines'. The text specifically states that the software runs on a 'personal computer' ('run all of the software on that computer'). Despite this discrepancy regarding the deployment hardware, the description is highly specific and detailed regarding the architectural style and integration.

The description states 'Not in Document'. The source text discusses communication between internal services via MQTT but does not mention integration with external systems, interoperability standards, or integration with other enterprise systems.

_A. Architecture_

_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)





















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.

block_3_judge.reasoning: Correctly identifies the CAD models, kinematic model, and the existence of a Simulation Service and Trajectory Generator Service. However, it incorrectly specifies the technology stack, citing 'InfluxDB/TimescaleDB' and 'virtual-machine images', whereas the source explicitly states the use of 'TimescaleDB' (based on PostgreSQL) and 'Docker' containerization, with virtual machine images mentioned only for a different exemplar (GreenhouseDT) in the related work section.

Correctly describes the motors for cart and hoist, their controllers, and the electromagnet. However, it incorrectly states the message broker is 'ActiveMQ' when the source specifies an 'MQTT Broker' (Eclipse Mosquitto). It also speculates that safety constraints are 'enforced by the controller firmware' without evidence, whereas the source describes a service that notifies operators when thresholds are breached.

Accurately lists the swing-angle encoder and anemometer. However, it incorrectly claims data is transmitted via 'MQTT/ActiveMQ' and stored in 'InfluxDB', while the source specifies 'MQTT Broker' (Mosquitto) and 'TimescaleDB'. Furthermore, it fabricates specific technical specifications (sampling rates, sub-degree accuracy, ±0.1 m/s accuracy) that are not present in the source text.

Accurately describes the Continuous Validation Service's function of comparing executed trajectories with simulations and checking distance metrics against thresholds. However, it adds unsupported details such as 'RMS error', 'uncertainty quantification', and 'automated error-correction loops'. The source specifically states that operators are notified via the dashboard to take appropriate action, rather than automated re-calibration occurring.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately describes the data flow from the crane's controllers through the Physical Crane Interface to the Logging Application and database. It correctly cites the continuous logging trigger and identifies the Python-based interface. However, it includes specific data types ('position, velocity, load') which are not explicitly listed in the text; the text only mentions logging the 'crane's state' and specific sensors like the 'Swing Angle Encoder' and 'Anemometer'.

Accurately describes the roles of the Control Application and the Trajectory Generator Service. It correctly notes the absence of detailed command protocols in the text. However, the statement that the Control Application 'consumes' trajectories from the generator is inferential as the text describes them separately without explicitly defining that data path in the provided snippets. Supported by quotes: 'Control Application: a Python application that uses the crane interface to control the crane' and 'Trajectory Generator Service: a service that utilizes a kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement trajectories for the crane'.

The extraction provides a highly accurate description of the technical connections, identifying the MQTT broker's role in signaling between services. It correctly notes the deployment on a single personal computer (implying a local host network) and the specific use of Docker for the broker, database, and dashboard. It also correctly identifies the absence of specific network topology details in the text.

The extraction is excellent, providing a comprehensive list of the logical containers as defined in the C4 model. It correctly distinguishes between the C4 'container' abstraction and the actual Docker containerization used for specific services (Database, Broker, Dashboard). It accurately describes the on-premise deployment on a personal computer and the lack of specific hardware specifications.

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses

3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)















Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
